## [00:00 - 03:00]

Alright guys, let's have a look at your funnels for e-commerce and also explore the concept of the minimum viable funnel. So generally speaking, when we're talking about funnels, we're talking about a process from going out and creating awareness of our products and then pulling people through the consideration phase in the marketing funnel where people are aware, you know, I need to lose weight and then they start considering their options. Do I get liposuction or do I get a personal trainer or do I get a gym membership? So taking them through the consideration phase, making them aware of your product, making them aware that you are a solution to their problem and then through to the purchase phase. So that's basically what a funnel does. It goes out there and it funnels leads into your business and turns them into customers. Now typically speaking, most people associate funnels with lead gen industry or online courses. However in e-commerce, we also have funnels and I'm going to actually take you through four of these today. You're also going to get blueprints for Lucidchart if you need templates to rule out in your business or for your contractors, etc., etc. Now generally speaking, when people are asking me about, you know, what kind of funnel should I be building for my client? It really comes down to the price point of their product. So the more expensive the product, generally speaking, the longer of a funnel you're going to need. It makes sense, right? If you're charging, you know, a couple hundred dollars for a product, people are going to want to really think about that and they're also going to really need to trust you and have that affinity with your brand or with yourself. Versus if it's a sort of five or twenty or fifty dollar product where it's not terribly high priced, you'll find a lot of impulse buyers in that segment. So you can get away with very, very short funnels. So that's a very big generalization. Now here's something super important about this, right? As you might be thinking, well, my client is selling seven dollar, I don't know, candles on their shop. So the price point is seven dollars. Well, this may not actually be the case. So it's important before you even get started to have a really good understanding on what is the actual price point of this product that you're working with.

## [03:00 - 06:04]

So when I use the term price point here, I'm actually mostly talking about average order value. This is one of the most important figures that you need to consider inside of your funnel. So the first thing to do is figure out or map out what is the actual lifetime value of a customer. So for example, if they're selling something where Tom uses this example once, tampons, for example, where every month the customer is going to have to buy from you again, well, the lifetime value of that customer may actually be quite high even though a box of tampons is only about, I don't know, 15 dollars, right? So what is the actual lifetime value? And it could be that the client or that yourself, if this is your own shop, is willing to actually break even on the initial purchase because you do have a high lifetime value of recurring payments on the back of that. The second thing to understand to really gain clarity on what an acceptable cost per acquisition is, is the average order value. And as I mentioned, this is one of the most important figures. So just because you're selling $7 candles on your shop, are people actually buying two or three on average of these candles? And is your average order value more around sort of $16 or $17, right? So you really need to figure out these figures. This is really important. If they're using Shopify, you can pull most of these through Shopify. Stripe will also give you an indication of lifetime value. And then finally, once you understand the average order value, then you can calculate what the target cost per acquisition is. So the CPA. And this is ideally in e-commerce around 30% of the average order value. So if you have an average order value of $100, your target cost per acquisition, meaning the target cost per conversions for purchases in your ad account should be $30 per purchase. That's what you would be aiming for overall. So this means top of funnel, you'll have a little bit more of a tolerance. And then bottom of funnel, they should come in way under that $30 mark. And overall, the entire funnel would balance out to a $30 cost per acquisition. So this is normally how you calculate this. This is really important to understand because if you have clients that are selling products under $50, it's really hard to make that work with Facebook ads unless it's what we call a kingpin product. However, if they have a higher average order value or if they have a higher lifetime value, it can be worth it for them. If they don't have a higher average order value, well, maybe that's a strategy that you need

## [06:04 - 09:07]

to map out with them. How do we actually increase that order value? Do we need to put plugins in place? How can we increase the lifetime value? So let's have a look at some typical e-commerce funnel. Now there's a lot of different variations on these guys and this is not the be all and end all, but these are generally four of the most common funnels that you'll see in the space. Now this is what we call pretty much the minimum viable funnel. I'll talk about this in a moment as well. This is where you go straight to a cold audience, sending them straight to the product page where it triggers a view content so it triggers the DPA and then you've got your retargeting in place ready to pick up any remaining sales. Now with this kind of funnel, you really want to see sales happening with your top of funnel ads and generally speaking, this works better for low price points as you saw in the graph. However, it can also work for higher price points as well. Second funnel example is pretty much the same as what we saw before except now at the very beginning, so with our cold audience, we're warming them up a little bit more with some content marketing. So this is normally a video. It could be a post, it could be an article that was published, so a piece of press, and then you're retargeting that audience with the actual product. So this one is just warming people up a little bit more. It tends to work with a little higher price point as well. Funnel number three is where you lead in with a lead magnet. So funnel number two is more of a soft content value based approach, whereas this one is definitely more of a lead magnet. So this could include a webinar, it could include a cheat sheet, a download, it could include an offer such as a free sample, it could even include a discount. So when you're leading in with, hey, you want to get 50% off this product, that is kind of a lead magnet at that stage. ## [09:07 - 12:10]

funnel number four is the same as number three, except we are prewarming our audiences even more at the top of the funnel. And then sending them through the lead magnet into the product page and then into the DPA or the retargeting stacks that we've set up. Again, this can work for a wide range of price points as well. So how do you know which funnel to choose and which funnel to get started with? Well, there's a lot of different theories out there on what you should do. And as you saw when we were looking at this graph, this can give you an idea, you can go, well, my price point is around here. So I need a fairly large funnel. However, after having seen so many e-commerce campaigns and run them myself, what I would recommend is I have actually seen the minimum viable funnel. I've seen ads that are direct response straight to a cold audience work phenomenally well. And especially if the client has great schema on their website or it's a type of product with high intent that Facebook just knows exactly who to go after, I've seen this funnel work really, really well. And so what I recommend is that you should always start with what we call the minimum viable funnel, direct response to a cold audience, sending straight to a view content product trigger, and then you're retargeting your DPA on the back of that, regardless of which price point you're going. Now what this is going to do for you is one or two things. The first is that if it works fantastic, you just saved yourself a ton of time and a ton of money trying to build a really long funnel that could have actually resulted in not getting any results for the client, because it was too convoluted. So if it works fantastic, you've just saved yourself a lot of time because you've started off with the minimum viable solution. Now if it doesn't work, now you have data, now you have data on where things are not going properly or where potential bottlenecks are happening. Now what you do if it doesn't work is really easy guys. All you start doing is you start building up your funnel, just like it's shown here. So you start going through these funnels. This is kind of from the easiest down through the hardest. So this one's going to take you quite a bit of work to set up. It's got a bit more content. You've got a lead magnet in there. So you basically start with the minimum viable funnel. If it works fantastic, if it doesn't, you start to beef it up. And what you're looking for there is obviously conversions. Is my top of funnel ad in my minimum viable funnel actually getting sales?
